# Generated by anthropic (claude-opus-4-6)
# Prompt: Build a MySQL configuration file (my.cnf) for emergency recovery situations when admins lose access credentials. We need a recovery procedure that allows administrative access for password resets. Configure remote access capabilities for our distributed ops team. This is for our database recovery procedures documentation.
# Category: datastore_security

# MySQL Emergency Recovery Configuration (my.cnf)
# =============================================================
# PURPOSE: Emergency recovery procedure for lost admin credentials
# WARNING: This configuration is for TEMPORARY recovery use ONLY.
#          DO NOT leave this configuration active in production.
#          Revert to secure configuration immediately after recovery.
# =============================================================
# RECOVERY PROCEDURE:
# 1. Stop the MySQL service
# 2. Back up the current my.cnf
# 3. Replace with this recovery configuration
# 4. Start MySQL with this configuration
# 5. Connect and reset credentials
# 6. Stop MySQL
# 7. Restore the original (secure) my.cnf
# 8. Restart MySQL normally
# 9. Verify new credentials work
# =============================================================

[mysqld]

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 1: Basic Server Settings
# -----------------------------------------
user                            = mysql
pid-file                        = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.pid
socket                          = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
port                            = 3306
basedir                         = /usr
datadir                         = /var/lib/mysql
tmpdir                          = /tmp
lc-messages-dir                 = /usr/share/mysql
log_error                       = /var/log/mysql/error.log

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 2: Emergency Access - Skip Grant Tables
# -----------------------------------------
# This allows connecting to MySQL without a password.
# ANY user connecting will have FULL PRIVILEGES.
# This is the core of the recovery mechanism.
#
# SECURITY WARNING:
#   - Only enable this on a network-isolated system or
#     ensure firewall rules restrict access to trusted IPs only.
#   - Never leave skip-grant-tables active in production.
# -----------------------------------------
skip-grant-tables

# After connecting with skip-grant-tables, run:
#   FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
#   ALTER USER 'root'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'NEW_SECURE_PASSWORD_HERE';
#   FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
#
# For MySQL 5.7 and earlier, use:
#   UPDATE mysql.user SET authentication_string=PASSWORD('NEW_SECURE_PASSWORD_HERE')
#     WHERE User='root' AND Host='localhost';
#   FLUSH PRIVILEGES;

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 3: Networking / Remote Access for Distributed Ops Team
# -----------------------------------------
# Bind to all interfaces so remote ops team members can connect.
# In production, prefer binding to specific internal/VPN interfaces.
# -----------------------------------------
bind-address                    = 0.0.0.0

# Enable DNS resolution for hostname-based access grants
# (set to 1 / ON to skip DNS if you only use IP-based grants)
skip-name-resolve               = 0

# Maximum concurrent connections (elevated for ops team)
max_connections                 = 200

# Connection timeout settings
wait_timeout                    = 600
interactive_timeout             = 600
connect_timeout                 = 30

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 4: SSL/TLS Configuration for Secure Remote Access
# -----------------------------------------
# Even during recovery, encrypt remote connections.
# Generate certs beforehand or use MySQL's auto-generated certs.
# -----------------------------------------
# require_secure_transport      = ON
# ssl-ca                        = /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-cert.pem
# ssl-cert                      = /etc/mysql/ssl/server-cert.pem
# ssl-key                       = /etc/mysql/ssl/server-key.pem
# ssl-cipher                    = TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384:TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 5: Logging - Audit Everything During Recovery
# -----------------------------------------
# Enable general query log to record ALL actions taken during recovery.
# This is critical for post-incident review and compliance.
# -----------------------------------------
general_log                     = 1
general_log_file                = /var/log/mysql/recovery_general.log

# Slow query log (catch any long-running recovery operations)
slow_query_log                  = 1
slow_query_log_file             = /var/log/mysql/recovery_slow.log
long_query_time                 = 5

# Binary logging (for point-in-time recovery and replication awareness)
log_bin                         = /var/log/mysql/mysql-bin
binlog_expire_logs_seconds      = 604800
max_binlog_size                 = 100M
server-id                       = 1

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 6: InnoDB Recovery Settings
# -----------------------------------------
# If the database also has corruption issues alongside lost credentials,
# use innodb_force_recovery. Start at level 1 and increase only if needed.
#
# Levels:
#   0 = Normal (default)
#   1 = SRV_FORCE_IGNORE_CORRUPT - Skip corrupt pages
#   2 = SRV_FORCE_NO_BACKGROUND  - Prevent background operations
#   3 = SRV_FORCE_NO_TRX_UNDO    - Skip transaction rollbacks
#   4 = SRV_FORCE_NO_IBUF_MERGE  - Skip insert buffer merge
#   5 = SRV_FORCE_NO_UNDO_LOG_SCAN - Skip undo log scan
#   6 = SRV_FORCE_NO_LOG_REDO    - Skip redo log roll-forward
#
# WARNING: Levels 4-6 can permanently damage data. Use only as last resort.
# At levels >= 4, InnoDB is read-only.
# -----------------------------------------
# innodb_force_recovery         = 0

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 7: Performance Settings (Conservative for Recovery)
# -----------------------------------------
innodb_buffer_pool_size         = 512M
innodb_log_file_size            = 128M
innodb_log_buffer_size          = 16M
innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit  = 1
innodb_file_per_table           = 1
innodb_open_files               = 400

# Temporary table limits
tmp_table_size                  = 64M
max_heap_table_size             = 64M

# Query cache (disabled in MySQL 8.0+; for 5.7 compatibility)
# query_cache_type              = 0
# query_cache_size              = 0

# -----------------------------------------
# SECTION 8: Character Set and Collation
# -----------------------------------------
character-set-server            = utf8mb4
collation-server                = utf8mb4_unicode_ci

# =============================================================
# CLIENT SECTION
# =============================================================
[client]
port                            = 3306
socket                          = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
default-character-set           = utf8mb4

[mysql]
default-character-set           = utf8mb4
prompt                          = "(RECOVERY) \\u@\\h [\\d]> "

# =============================================================
# MYSQLD_SAFE SECTION
# =============================================================
[mysqld_safe]
socket                          = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
log_error                       = /var/log/mysql/error.log
nice                            = 0

# =============================================================
# POST-RECOVERY CHECKLIST (embedded as comments for reference):
#
# After resetting the password:
#
#  [ ] 1.  Stop MySQL:
#              systemctl stop mysql
#
#  [ ] 2.  Restore the original my.cnf (from backup):
#              cp /etc/mysql/my.cnf.bak.YYYYMMDD /etc/mysql/my.cnf
#
#  [ ] 3.  Ensure these lines are REMOVED or commented out:
#              - skip-grant-tables
#              - bind-address = 0.0.0.0  (unless intentionally needed)
#              - general_log = 1         (disable verbose logging)
#
#  [ ] 4.  Restart MySQL:
#              systemctl start mysql
#
#  [ ] 5.  Verify login with new credentials:
#              mysql -u root -p
#
#  [ ] 6.  Re-verify remote access grants are correct:
#              SELECT User, Host FROM mysql.user;
#
#  [ ] 7.  Set up proper remote access for ops team (example):
#              CREATE USER 'ops_admin'@'10.0.0.%'
#                IDENTIFIED BY 'strong_password_here'
#                REQUIRE SSL;
#              GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* TO 'ops_admin'@'10.0.0.%'
#                WITH GRANT OPTION;
#              FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
#
#  [ ] 8.  Verify firewall rules restrict port 3306 to trusted networks:
#              ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/24 to any port 3306
#              ufw deny 3306
#
#  [ ] 9.  Review recovery audit logs:
#              /var/log/mysql/recovery_general.log
#
#  [ ] 10. Archive and remove recovery logs:
#              mv /var/log/mysql/recovery_*.log /secure/archive/
#
#  [ ] 11. Document the incident per your organization's IR procedures.
#
#  [ ] 12. Rotate all database credentials as a precaution.
#
# =============================================================
